title: The Last Dowry
blurb: A grieving heiress stitches vengeance into every seam. The man who owns her future smiles—while his sister threads a silk noose around his empire. Black lace hides ledgers. Ice holds fire.
comparable: For fans of *The Gilded Wolves* by Roshani Chokshi, *Tangled in Silk* by Loretta Chase

chapters.text:
Chapter 1

The Marble Ledger House smelled of lemon oil and old secrets. Claretta Valente Velsanti paused beneath a chandelier of frosted crystal, the black silk of her gown drinking in the pale light from the streetlamps outside. She moved like a shadow across the checkerboard floor—one step, then another, fingers trailing the spine of a ledger left carelessly open on an empty desk.

A pencil snapped.

She turned. Bookkeeper Orsini stood in the doorway, his starched collar unbuttoned at the throat, ink smudging the pad of his thumb.

“You’re standing on Syndicate property, Contessa,” he said, not raising his voice. “That means these books are mine to supervise.”

Retta withdrew her hand from the ledger. Her fingertips were stained with graphite and silver thread. “Of course. I was only confirming the balance for the next auction cycle.”

Orsini’s gaze dropped to the ledger’s margins. Tiny silver threads curled along the paper’s edge, stitching patterns only she could read. He reached out, plucked a loose page from her sleeve before she could react, and held it up between two fingers.

“This isn’t signed,” he said. “No initials, no stamp. Just… embroidery.”

Retta’s pulse slowed. “A decorative flourish. My mother’s habit.”

“Your mother’s been dead twelve years.” Orsini folded the page into his waistcoat pocket without asking. “You overstep, Contessa. Again.”

She didn’t move. Didn’t flinch. The silver threads in the ledger’s margin pulsed faintly in the lamplight—names, dates, routes. Illicit trade. Proof. All stitched in living thread, never ink.

“Apologies,” she said. “I’ll take my leave.”

Orsini stepped aside, but not before his eyes flicked to the hem of her skirt, where a single silver thread glinted against the black silk. He said nothing.

She glided past him into the vaulted corridor, her skirts whispering against the marble. The air grew colder as she approached the Archives—an antechamber of iron shelves and vaulted ceilings, where the Syndicate’s true ledgers were kept under lock and key. She knew every seam in her gown had been measured for concealment. Every shadow in the House had been mapped.

She was halfway down the aisle of iron shelves when the clerk emerged from the shadows.

A young man, barely twenty, with a ledger of his own tucked under his arm. He cleared his throat.

“Madam,” he said, “you’re not authorized in this section.”

Retta didn’t look at him. “I’m conducting a private audit.”

“On whose authority?”

“My father’s.” The words hung in the still air, heavy with what they implied. The clerk’s fingers tightened around his ledger. “The Syndicate charter grants the Velsanti name oversight of all audit trails prior to the fire.”

The clerk hesitated. Then, reluctantly, he stepped aside.

She passed him, brushing the silk of her sleeve against his arm. A coded signal. He didn’t react.

In the Archives, she knelt before the iron-bound ledger of the Winter Silk Syndicate. The cover was embossed with the Syndicate crest—a silver loom threading a frozen rose. She pressed her palm to the metal. Cold seeped through her gloves.

She opened the book.

Inside, the margins were bare. Empty. Waiting.

Retta removed a silver thimble from her reticule. With practiced fingers, she unraveled a thread from the inner hem of her gown. It glinted like wire in the lamplight—stronger than silk, thinner than wire. She fed it through the eye of her needle.

The first stitch went in clean.

Behind her, a floorboard creaked.

She didn’t turn. Didn’t pause. But her hand stilled.

The clerk stood in the doorway, watching. His breath hitched. “Contessa,” he said, “you’re stitching into *our* ledger.”

Retta kept her eyes on the page. “I’m completing what was started.”

“That’s not—”

“You signed the transfer of assets after the fire,” she said, voice low. “You saw the payout. You know it exceeded the damage. The Syndicate’s insurance was liquidated into offshore trusts. My father’s fortune. Mine.”

The clerk’s face went slack. “I—I didn’t—”

“You did.” She pulled the needle through, tight and precise. “You all did.”

Silence settled. The ledger’s pages rustled in the draft.

Then—footsteps.

Orsini reappeared, his waistcoat bulging slightly where the folded page pressed against his chest.

“Contessa,” he said, smooth as oil, “I believe you’ve concluded your business.”

Retta stood. The ledger snapped shut beneath her touch.

She turned to face them both—Orsini, the clerk, the vault of secrets.

“Not nearly,” she said.

Orsini smiled. “Then allow me to assist.”

He stepped closer. Too close. His fingers brushed her wrist as he reached past her for the ledger.

Retta let him take it.

But as she turned to leave, her skirt swayed.

And from the folds of black silk, a single page drifted to the floor—untouched, unnoticed.

Unmissed.

Orsini stooped to pick it up.

Retta walked away, her heels clicking against the marble, her stitches pulsing faintly in the dark.

She didn’t look back.

She didn’t need to.

Chapter 2

The Frost Chapel smelled of beeswax and frostbitten roses. Black silk rippled across the marble floor like spilled ink, pooling around Retta’s knees. She knelt at the altar, the embroidered hem of her gown brushing the first step of the dais. Above her, the Syndicate brass policed the aisles, their cuffs catching the winter light that slanted through stained glass.

Darian Rook stood beside her. His coat was charcoal, his gloves pale. A single silver thread glinted at his lapel—uniform for tonight’s gala. He stared straight ahead, jaw set, as though she were a prop.

Archbishop Vero cleared his throat. “Beloved in trust and trade, we gather tonight beneath the eyes of the Syndicate’s patroness, the Lady of Loom and Ledger—”

Retta’s gloved hand lifted, instinctive.

Darian’s palm shot out. He intercepted her fingers halfway between them, trapping her wrist against his thigh. His grip was firm enough to bruise.

“Don’t,” he murmured. “You’ll wrinkle the sleeve.”

The Syndicate gasped. Livia Sable’s gloved hand twitched in her lap. Elio D’Annunzio’s wine did not.

Retta stilled. The needlework along her forearm—a cipher of dates and routes—pulsed faintly beneath black silk. It was the first she’d touched him since the engagement was announced. His cufflink bit into her wrist, cold and precise.

“Of course,” she said. Her voice carried. “We wouldn’t want to mar the evening’s perfection.”

Darian did not look at her. “This is a merger, not a romance. Remember that.”

Archbishop Vero blinked. “Shall we proceed with the vows?”

Retta lowered her hand. The chapel’s stained glass threw ruby and cobalt fractals across her skirts. She folded her fingers into her palm and waited. One. Two. Three seconds. The Syndicate’s scrutiny settled like dust.

Elio set down his wineglass with a quiet click. He stood, then strode forward, tucking a white handkerchief into his cuff. He was Retta’s height, silver-haired, his face carved from old bone. He leaned toward the altar and laid a hand on Darian’s shoulder.

“Brother,” he said, loud enough for the room, “the Syndicate expects unity.”

Darian’s jaw tightened. “Then the Syndicate should have picked someone less… *hands-on*.”

The room chilled. Retta felt Livia’s gaze flick to her sleeve again—the cipher stitches, the silver threads that spelled more than mourning.

Elio didn’t flinch. “Contessa Valente is a woman of depth. Her grief is a testament to fidelity.”

“Her grief is a crutch,” Darian said. “And we don’t do crutches in winter commerce.”

Archbishop Vero coughed into his stole. “Shall we—”

“Continue?” Retta finished, voice serene. She unfolded her fingers, letting the light catch the silver threads along her wrist. “By all means. But let’s not pretend this is a union of hearts. Hearts aren’t traded in the Winter Silk Syndicate. Only ledgers are.”

Darian exhaled through his nose. “Spoken like someone who’s never run a mill.”

“And you speak like someone who’s never lost one.” She tilted her head slightly, letting the light strip the mourning dye from her lashes. “Or discovered the ledgers that proved it.”

A beat of silence. The Syndicate held its breath.

Elio’s smile stayed smooth. “Sentiment before the auction? Unwise. The Syndicate values discipline.”

Livia stood. Her embroidered shawl—woven with silver thread, familiar patterns—slid from her shoulders. She draped it over Darian’s arm without a word. He accepted it automatically, his fingers brushing hers. A flicker of tension. A private language.

“Thank you,” he said.

Livia didn’t look at him. Her dark eyes locked onto Retta’s sleeve. “The stitching,” she said quietly, almost to herself. “It’s… precise.”

Retta kept her hands folded. “My mother’s influence. She taught me needlework before she taught me numbers.”

Darian draped the shawl over his other arm. “Numbers are safer.”

Livia’s fingers twitched at her sides. “So are scissors.”

A ripple of laughter passed through the Syndicate. Archbishop Vero forged ahead. “Shall we begin the—”

Retta interrupted. “I believe the Syndicate values transparency. Isn’t that what we’re here for? To see the ledgers, the routes, the truth before the auction?”

Darian’s face darkened. “The auction is irrelevant. What matters is the presidency. And the presidency belongs to the one who controls the trade routes.”

“Control,” Retta said, “isn’t ownership. It’s theft dressed in silk.”

Elio stepped between them. “Enough. The vows.”

Retta rose in one fluid motion. The black silk of her gown whispered against the marble. She met Darian’s gaze, steady and unblinking. “I will. But only after the Syndicate sees the truth stitched into the margins of its own books.”

Darian’s glove crunched slightly. “You’re stitching accusations into mourning gowns now?”

“Into the future,” she said. “Every seam is a contract. Every thread a record.”

Livia’s breath hitched. She took a half-step forward. “Contessa—”

Elio’s hand settled on Livia’s elbow, sharp and proprietary. “Enough theatre. Begin the ceremony.”

Archbishop Vero began the Latin. The Syndicate bowed their heads. Darian folded his arms, his profile rigid. Retta knelt again, her skirt pooling around her. She kept her hands clasped, serene.

But beneath the black silk, her fingers curled.

The silver threads along her sleeves pulsed once, twice—a silent code. The cipher was nearly complete. The ledger in the Archives was burning.

And the shawl draped over Darian’s arm carried a pattern only she could finish.

Livia’s eyes flickered between Retta’s sleeve and the shawl. Recognition. Fear. Hunger.

Elio’s eyes flicked to Retta’s sleeve, then to the page in his pocket—the one she’d slipped past Orsini.

His lips parted.

Recognition not of the act, but of the stitching.

The Syndicate murmured the final amen.

Darian turned to take his place at the altar. His coat flared, the shawl sliding from his arm.

Livia caught it before it hit the floor.

Her fingers trembled.

And Retta smiled.

Not at Darian. Not at Elio.

At the silver threads in the shawl’s hem—threads that matched the cipher in her sleeve.

The stitching was hers.

And the Syndicate had just watched her finish it.

Chapter 3

The Obsidian Vault smelled of old ink and polished stone. The air was cool, thick with the scent of aged parchment and the faint metallic tang of stored silver. Retta moved through the shadows, her black silk gown whispering against the marble floor. The false panel was small, tucked behind a stack of ledgers bound in oxblood leather. She knelt, slipping a hairpin from the coiled knot at the nape of her neck. The pin was silver, delicate—her mother’s. She had kept it hidden in the heel of a boot for years.

Her fingers worked with practiced precision. The lock was old, but not broken. Elio had trusted the mechanism’s integrity, just as he trusted Retta’s grief. She felt the pin slide into the keyhole, heard the faint click as the catch gave way. The panel swung inward on silent hinges.

Beyond lay a narrow cavity. Retta reached inside, her palm brushing against a stack of papers tied with a ribbon the color of dried blood. She pulled the bundle free, her breath steady. The first page was brittle at the edges, the ink faded but legible. It was a ledger fragment—one of the insurance payouts for the Gilded Hive fire. The numbers were clear: Rook & Sable had claimed twice the loss, and the discrepancy was impossible to ignore.

She folded the page carefully, tucking it into the hidden pocket sewn into the lining of her gown. The seam held—barely. Her fingers checked the stitches, ensuring the hem remained intact. One loose thread could unravel everything.

A floorboard creaked above her.

Retta froze. The sound was distant, but in the silence of the Vault, it carried. She exhaled slowly, turning her head toward the staircase. No one came down. The Syndicate’s archives were accessed only by keyed doors, and the key was in Elio’s pocket. But she had learned long ago that keys could be copied, and doors could be bypassed.

She stood, her skirts settling around her. The Vault’s single lantern cast long shadows, making the shelves of ledgers look like rows of silent sentinels. Retta ran her fingers along the spines of the nearest volumes, pretending to study their titles. *Trade Routes of Valtoria. Dye Guild Ledgers. Syndicate Minutes, 1895.* None of them were what she needed.

The creak came again—closer this time. Boots on marble. Not the soft slip of silk or the whisper of a gown, but the deliberate strike of a man’s heel.

She turned just as Elio D’Annunzio descended the final step. His coat was dark, his gloves pristine. He held a glass of port in one hand, the liquid catching the lantern light like polished bronze. His eyes met hers, and for a moment, neither spoke.

“Contessa,” he said at last. “The Syndicate wonders at your absence.”

Retta kept her hands clasped before her. “The Syndicate also wonders at many things, Marquise. Tonight, it seems, it is my grief that draws their curiosity.”

Elio took a slow sip of his drink. “Grief can be a heavy cloak.”

“But it keeps one warm,” she replied. “And prevents others from noticing the chill in the air.”

He set the glass down on a nearby pedestal, the clink sharp in the quiet. “You’ve been in the Vault for an hour.”

“Time moves differently when one is surrounded by ghosts.”

Elio exhaled through his nose, a sound that might have been amusement or exasperation. He stepped closer, his polished shoes clicking against the stone. “Elio,” she corrected softly. “We aren’t in company.”

“No,” he agreed. “We aren’t.”

His hand moved toward his coat pocket, and Retta tensed. But he only adjusted his cuff, the silver thread glinting faintly in the dim light. The same thread from the engagement gala. The same thread she had stitched into his cuffs months ago, a silent marker for the Syndicate’s inner circle.

“You’re quiet,” he observed. “Too quiet.”

“I find noise overrated.”

“And yet, sound carries in here.” He tilted his head toward the staircase. “Darian’s upstairs, reviewing the auction bids. He asked after you.”

Retta allowed herself a small, humorless smile. “Did he?”

“He’s concerned.”

“He lies.”

Elio’s lips thinned. “He’s pragmatic. And pragmatism is a virtue in trade.”

“A virtue that masks theft.”

His gaze flicked to her sleeves, to the silver threads woven into the mourning black. “You’re still stitching.”

“Am I?”

“You were always better with a needle than with people.”

“And you were always better at pretending kindness than practicing it.”

Elio’s fingers twitched at his side. “You’ve been in the dark too long, Contessa.”

“I’ve been in the light too long. And all I saw was shadows wearing robes of silk.”

A silence settled between them, heavier than the stone around them. Elio’s eyes darkened. “You won’t break him.”

Retta tilted her head. “I’m not here to break Darian.”

“Then why are you here?”

“To reclaim what’s mine.”

He laughed, low and humorless. “You have no proof. No ledgers. No witnesses.”

“But I have a hairpin,” she said. “And a false panel. And a ledger fragment that proves your insurance fraud to the Syndicate elders.”

Elio’s smile vanished. His hand drifted toward his pocket again—toward the key, perhaps, or the pistol she knew he carried when nerves were thin.

“You’re bluffing.”

“I don’t bluff.”

He took a step closer. “Then what are you doing, Retta?”

The use of her given name was a mistake. A crack in his composure. She saw it in the way his jaw tightened, in the flicker of something like fear in his eyes.

“I’m finishing the ledger,” she said.

“There is no ledger.”

“There is now.”

His hand shot out, gripping her wrist. The port glass teetered on the pedestal, then crashed to the floor, the sound shattering against the marble. “You’re playing a dangerous game.”

Retta didn’t pull away. “I’m playing the only game left.”

Elio’s fingers dug into her skin, but she didn’t flinch. “Darian will ruin you.”

“Darian has already ruined himself. He just doesn’t know it yet.”

A beat. A breath. Then Elio released her so suddenly she nearly lost her balance. He stepped back, his coat flaring. “You’ll regret this.”

“I already do.”

He turned toward the staircase, then paused. “Elio.”

He didn’t look back.

“You were like a father to me,” she said quietly. “And I trusted you. I trusted the Syndicate. But fathers sometimes deceive. And Syndicates sometimes burn.”

He didn’t answer. He didn’t need to.

Retta watched him climb the stairs, the sound of his boots fading into the marble. The Vault swallowed him whole.

She waited until the silence returned. Then she reached into her hidden pocket and pulled out the ledger fragment again. The numbers glared up at her, bold and undeniable. Rook & Sable had inflated their insurance claim by 200%. The excess had been funneled into offshore trusts—trusts she controlled.

She folded the paper carefully, tucking it back into place. Then she ran her fingers along the spines of the ledgers once more, stopping at a volume bound in deep blue. She pulled it free, flipping to the last page. There, in silver thread, was the cipher she had been stitching for months. The final entry.

The Syndicate’s presidency was awarded to the one who controlled the ledgers.

And tonight, she had reclaimed hers.

Chapter 4

The Gilded Hive’s war room smelled of beeswax and ambition. The long table was polished mahogany, scored with the faintest grooves—where knives had once been tested, or bribes pressed into palms. Syndicate elders sat in high-backed chairs of carved oak, their coats dark enough to absorb the gaslight bleeding through the frosted glass. At the head, Darian Rook stood with his back to the door, sleeves rolled to the elbow, fingers steepled on the table.

Retta entered behind Marquise Elio. She wore black, as always. The gown’s hem whispered against the floor, a sound like a blade being drawn from a sheath. No one turned to look at her. They didn’t have to. She was a ghost in mourning, a cipher in stitched silence.

Elio cleared his throat. “Gentlemen. Darian.”

Darian turned slowly, his face a mask of controlled satisfaction. “Marquise. And Contessa.” His gaze flicked over Retta without recognition, as if she were a mannequin in a shop window. “I wasn’t aware you’d joined us.”

“Her presence is a courtesy,” Elio said, stepping to the side. “The Syndicate values transparency.”

“Transparency is an indulgence,” said Elder Voss, a man whose hands were gnarled from decades of dye vats. He tapped his cane against the floor. “We prefer results.”

Darian smiled. “Then we’re in agreement.”

Retta remained beside Elio, her hands clasped. The silver thread in Darian’s right cuff caught the light—her own stitching, a private mark. She had embroidered it on the night of their engagement, a single silver knot at the edge. A lover’s joke. A spy’s signature.

Elio leaned toward her. “You needn’t stay.”

“I needn’t,” she replied. “But I do.”

Darian unfolded a ledger, the pages stiff with starch and secrecy. “The winter silk shipment from the northern mills has arrived ahead of schedule. Quality is unmatched. A triumph of design, thanks to my sister’s vision.” He nodded toward the far end of the table where Livia Sable sat, her sketchbook open, a silver needle flashing between gloved fingers. “She’s even incorporated Retta’s signature stitch—though I doubt the Contessa would approve of such theft.”

Livia didn’t look up. Her pen moved in sharp, precise strokes. “Silk doesn’t care who stitches it, only how.”

Elder Miro, flaccid-cheeked and slow of tongue, leaned forward. “A bold claim. Thread has loyalties.”

“So do women,” said Livia, finally lifting her gaze. It lingered—not on Darian, but on Retta. Recognition flickered in her eyes, gone before it settled. “Some more than others.”

Darian chuckled. “Spoken like a true Sable. But loyalty is earned.”

“Or inherited,” Retta said softly.

The room stilled. The elders shifted in their seats. Elio’s jaw tightened.

Darian’s smile didn’t waver. He lifted his cuff, displaying the silver thread. “A gift from your late father, I believe.” He ran a thumb over the knot. “He and I shared many threads.”

“You shared his lies,” Retta said.

The words hung. The air thickened.

Elder Voss coughed. “Contessa, we are here to discuss the auction.”

“The auction,” Darian repeated, “is already decided. The presidency goes to the steward of the finest silk. That is me.” He gestured to Livia. “And my sister, who ensures no stain mars our reputation.”

Livia set down her needle. Her hands were steady, but her lips pressed into a thin line. “Reputation is fragile, brother.”

Darian’s gaze sharpened. “Careful. You’re not the lead designer here. You’re the heiress’s dressmaker.”

“And you’re the man wearing a widow’s stitch on your cuff,” Livia said.

A beat. A breath. The elders exhaled in unison.

Darian’s fingers flexed. Then he laughed, low and rich. “A jest. Very well. Let us proceed.” He turned to the head of the table. “The ledgers are flawless. The silk is flawless. The Syndicate will not be disappointed.”

Voss cleared his throat. “And if the Contessa’s ledgers are not?”

Darian’s smile didn’t flicker. “She has no ledgers. Only black silk and a sharp tongue.”

Retta stepped forward. “Ledgers are records. Tongues are weapons.”

Elio’s hand twitched toward his pocket. Not for a key this time, but for reassurance. He had believed her broken. He had been wrong.

Darian leaned back, fingers laced. “You sound like a woman who’s lost.”

“I sound like a woman who’s won,” Retta said.

Silence. Then, from the far end, Livia spoke again. “You’re both bluffing.”

Darian’s head snapped toward his sister. “Livia.”

“She’s wearing a ledger on her sleeve,” Livia said, nodding at the hem of Retta’s gown. “The mourning stitches aren’t just embroidery. They’re code. And beneath them, she’s hiding something far worse than pride.”

The room darkened. The gaslight flickered.

Darian’s eyes moved from Retta to the gown, to the silver threads shimmering like spider silk across the black fabric. His fingers tightened on the table edge.

Retta did not move. She let them see her. Let them stare.

Elder Voss whispered, “A Velsanti without a ledger is a ghost. She’ll fade.”

Retta smiled. “Ghosts don’t fade. They haunt.”

Darian’s voice cut through the tension, sharp as a blade. “Elio. Contessa. Leave us.”

Elio didn’t hesitate. He took Retta’s elbow, steering her toward the door. She allowed it. Her gaze lingered on Livia, who had gone still, her needle poised above the page like a weapon raised and then lowered.

As they reached the threshold, Retta glanced back.

Livia’s eyes met hers.

And in that look, Retta saw something tremble—not fear, not guilt, but recognition. As if Livia had just glimpsed a reflection she did not recognize, and could not unsee.

The door swung shut behind them.

In the corridor, Elio’s grip was iron. “Don’t speak again.”

“You’re afraid of a ledger?” she asked.

“Of what it proves.”

She let him lead her down the hall, her skirts whispering behind them like a secret already told.

Chapter 5

The Marble Ledger House breathed as only old institutions did—through whispers in marble corridors and the slow grind of power beneath its floors. The auction chamber was a vault of polished stone, its circular dais ringed with gaslights that pooled in golden pools on the dark teak floor. Syndicate heads perched like crows on gilded chairs, their coats the color of damp wool and old tea. They had come to buy silk, not trust.

Darian stood at the rostrum, his coat cut sharp enough to split seams. The ledger in his hand gleamed under the light, its pages crisp as new currency. He tapped the spine once with his knuckle.

“Gentlemen,” he said, “tonight, we redefine fortune.”

Retta leaned against a column in the rear gallery, her black gown swallowing the shadows behind her. The silk lining rustled like a held breath. She had not been invited. She had not expected to come.

At the center of the dais, a single bolt of winter silk lay draped over a mahogany stand. The fabric shimmered, a virginal white shot through with threads of silver-green—colors meant to evoke the northern mines, the heart of Velsanti legacy. The crowd murmured, fingers twitching toward notebooks.

Darian gestured to his sister. “Livia?”

She stood from her seat near the front, her skirts whispering against the floor. Her hands were bare this evening, the better to display the precision of her craft. She lifted one corner of the silk.

The room stilled.

A stain bloomed at the fold—a rust-brown splotch, spreading like a bruise. The gaslights dimmed, not from flicker but from collective inhale.

Darian’s face did not change. “A mere blemish. Easily cut away.”

Elder Voss rose, his cane tapping once against the floor. “Blemishes in silk are portents. You know that, Rook. A flaw in the weave invites the eye to the flaw in the ledger. And I, for one, do not care to see my fortune unravel in front of me.”

The room hummed. Men shifted. Notebooks snapped shut.

“This silk was inspected at the Hive,” Darian said. “Tested. Verified.”

“Then why does it bleed?” asked Miro, the guildmaster of the dye houses, his fingers steepled under his chin.

Livia’s jaw tightened. She touched the stain with two fingers, lifted them to the light. “It’s not dye. It’s oxidation. The silver threads reacted with something acidic.” Her voice was steady, trained. “This silk has been exposed to ledger ink.”

A gasp. Not from the gallery, but from the dais.

Darian’s eyes narrowed. “A trick of the light.”

“No trick,” said Livia. She stepped closer, exposing the stain. “Look. The threads are frayed where it touched. This silk was wrapped around a ledger. A wet one.”

The elders leaned in. The truth settled like dust.

Retta watched from the shadows. She had not touched the bolt. But she had given the order. The Clerk from Chapter One, a gaunt man with ink-stained cuffs, had slipped the bolt into a tannic bath before it left the vault. The acid in the water had been weak, slow—enough to mar the silk, not ruin it. Enough to make the Syndicate hesitate.

Darian’s fingers curled into his palm. “Contessa Velsanti was not invited to this auction,” he said, voice soft. “But she is here. In her mourning. In her silence.”

Retta did not answer. She didn’t have to.

Livia turned, her gaze sweeping the gallery. It found Retta. Paused. The gallery’s dim light caught the silver in Retta’s cuffs—her cipher, stitched in secret. The same thread that had marked Darian’s cuff weeks ago. Livia’s eyes flickered recognition.

“She’s wearing the ledger,” Livia said quietly. “On her sleeve.”

Darian followed her gaze. His breath stopped for a heartbeat.

The stain. The cipher. The ledger on Retta’s hem.

He did not shout. Did not storm. He simply stood, rigid, the ledger still clutched in his hand like a relic.

Miro broke the silence. “Rook. You said the silk was flawless.”

Darian did not look away from Retta. “It was.”

“Then why does it weep?”

The room tensed. The air thickened with the scent of beeswax, wool, and old fear.

Retta lifted her hand. She did not wave. Did not smile. She simply drew a slow breath, filling the black silk of her bodice. The ledger stitches along her sleeve tensed, caught the light.

Darian’s voice cut through the dark. “Elio. Find her.”

The Marquise emerged from the side door, his face pale beneath his silver hair. He moved quickly, too quickly, toward the stairs. His gloves were too tight. His coat too heavy.

Retta watched him descend. He would not meet her eyes. When he reached the aisle, he stopped. His hand went to his pocket. Not for a key. For a note.

He slipped it into Darian’s pocket with a gesture so practiced it could have been a kiss of death.

Darian felt it. His fingers twitched. He reached for his breast pocket.

The stamp of the note showed through the fabric—a single silver thread.

*Your ledger stitches are showing.*

Darian’s hand stilled.

The Syndicate elders did not see the note. They saw only the stain on the silk. The silence in the room. The way the Marquise’s shoulders slumped as if he carried the weight of lead.

And they knew.

This was not a sale.

It was an ambush.

Chapter 6

The Frost Chapel stood behind the Marble Ledger House like a ghost of weddings past—a place where vows were carved into stone and dowries were buried in haste. Its iron-bound doors were painted the color of dried blood. The hinges groaned when Retta pushed them open, as if the chapel itself had been waiting.

Tonio waited inside, his cap crumpled in hands rough as bark. He had once loaded bales of silk for the Velsanti trade route before the marquise had "reassigned" him to the docks. Now he loaded secrets.

Retta stepped over the threshold. The air smelled of candle wax and old incense, the scent of men who had prayed over ledgers, not gods. Her heels clicked against the marble. The priest—Father Abelli—stood beside the altar, his chasuble wrinkled at the elbows. He had agreed to this meeting in exchange for a donation to the orphanage, never asking what was in the chest.

She set the chest down.

It was iron-bound, the size of a small footlocker, its surface etched with the Velsanti crest—a silver tree on black enamel. The lock was new, its keyhole small and precise. She had commissioned it herself, in the merchant quarter, using no name, only coin.

Tonio exhaled through his nose. “You’re sure he won’t look?”

“He won’t have time,” she said.

The priest lifted the chest lid a finger’s width, then closed it. “Blessed be the weight you carry, Contessa.”

Retta did not answer. The priest was a man who blessed ledgers more than souls.

She turned to leave.

Tonio cleared his throat. “The lock—”

“Is mine,” she said. “Keep it that way.”

He nodded, jaw tight, and pulled a folded note from his waistcoat. “Address is sewn into the lining. And the chest bears your cipher. He’ll see it.”

Retta took the note, unfolded it with one finger. The paper was cheap, the ink smudged. A tavern name. A time. A single word at the bottom: *Burn.*

She folded it again. “If he asks.”

Tonio’s lips pressed thin. “He won’t ask. He’ll know.”

She dropped the note into his palm. Their fingers brushed. No warmth passed between them—only trust, and the memory of bales that had once borne silk, not proof.

The chapel door swung shut behind her.

---

Marble Ledger House, third floor—Elio’s office smelled of lemon oil and old wool. The marquise stood by the window, his back to the door, watching the winter square below where carriages jostled for space like impatient horses. His fingers drummed against the windowsill.

Retta did not knock.

The door creaked open. He did not turn.

“You left the chapel too soon,” he said. “The priest looked troubled.”

She closed the door behind her.

Elio turned, his silver hair catching the lamplight. His cuffs were pristine, the lace so fine it might have been spun from frost. He had not yet felt the weight of the chest. Or perhaps he had, and this was his rehearsal.

“Troubled men are useful,” she said. “They remember who paid them.”

He stepped forward, his boots silent on the Persian rug. “You’re playing a dangerous game with symbols, Contessa. A cipher on a lock is not a dowry. It’s a confession.”

Retta walked past him, her skirts trailing over the rug. She stopped at his desk, where a ledger lay open—an account of the Rook & Sable autumn dye shipments. The ink was fresh. Her cipher was not.

“You always overestimate your ability to read me,” she said. “I didn’t leave the chest in the chapel to hide it. I left it there to mark my property.”

Elio’s gaze flicked to the door. “Property that risks everything.”

“Property that reclaims it.”

He exhaled sharply through his nose. “Darian will search every vault, every ledger room. He’ll find it.”

“Not if it’s already gone.”

“Gone where?”

Retta did not answer. Instead, she reached into her sleeve and withdrew a small silk pouch. She placed it on the desk between them. Inside, three keys—brass, iron, silver.

Elio’s fingers twitched. He did not touch them.

“Keys to the Obsidian Vault,” she said. “To the Frost Chapel crypt. To a ledger chest in the Gilded Hive basement.”

His breath hitched—just once.

Retta watched his face. The marquise had taught her how to read ledgers. She was learning how to read people.

“You think I handed you a future,” he said slowly. “I gave you a prison.”

She lifted one shoulder. The black silk of her sleeve rode up, exposing the edge of her cuff. Her cipher glinted in the lamplight—tiny silver stitches spelling her name in a dead language.

Elio’s gaze locked onto it. His face drained of color, not with fear, but with recognition.

“That stitch,” he whispered.

Retta did not move. “It is.”

He reached for the pouch. His fingers were steady. But his breath came faster.

He did not open it.

Instead, he lifted the pouch and set it in her hands. “You misunderstand. I didn’t fake trust for years. I faked obedience. And now you’ve undone me.”

She felt his pulse through the silk of the pouch—fast, like a thread fraying.

Elio stepped back. His boot caught the leg of a chair. The chair skidded. The ledger on the desk flipped shut with a slap.

“You’re not the only one who knows where the vaults are,” he said softly. “The Syndicate elders know. Darian knows. His sister—” He stopped. Swallowed. “His sister stitches too.”

Retta’s fingers tightened around the pouch.

Elio’s voice dropped lower. “Livia noticed your stitch. Days ago. She’s been watching you. Waiting to see if you’d unravel.”

The air in the room thickened. The scent of lemon and wool turned sour.

Retta turned toward the door.

Elio did not call her back.

She walked out.

The hallway stretched before her, cold and silent. At the far end, a draft whispered through the ventilation shafts. She heard it, felt it—like the breath of a man who had just been dealt a losing hand.

She did not look back.

But she knew.

Elio had seen the cipher on the chest’s lock. He had understood.

And in that moment, Retta knew something worse.

Darian’s sister had already seen it too.

Chapter 7

The Gilded Hive pulsed like a living thing. A thousand lanterns bobbed in the rafters, their glass painted with silk patterns that shifted red and gold in the heat of gaslight. The air smelled of candle wax, crushed jasmine, and the sharp metallic tang of dye thinners seeping from the lower mills. Retta stood at the edge of the ballroom, gloved hands folded over the black silk of her mourning gown. The fabric was stiff with hidden seams—ledgers waiting to be read.

Around her, Syndicate elders in velvet coats mumbled about auction strategy. Darian held court near the dais, his arm hooked around the elbow of an older man in a burgundy sash—Head of the Dye Guild, if the embroidery on his lapel was to be believed. His laughter cut through the murmur, too loud, too practiced.

Retta did not look at him.

Livia moved through the crowd like a ghost in white silk, her auburn hair caught up in a net of silver thread. She carried a sketchbook under one arm, its cover cracked with age. Every so often she paused to murmur to a seamstress or dip a finger into a bowl of dye water, testing shades for the winter collection.

Retta watched her.

A gong rang from the dais. The chatter died.

Darian raised a glass. “Gentlemen—and ladies—thank you for another year of prosperity.”

The crowd murmured assent. Retta kept her face still.

“Before we drink,” Darian continued, “I must ask: has anyone seen Marquise Elio? He was supposed to present the dye guild’s report.”

A ripple of uncertainty passed through the room. Elders exchanged glances. Someone coughed.

Retta felt the weight of silence settle over the hall.

Then, from the back—an elder in a high-collared coat stood too quickly, his chair scraping. “Contessa Velsanti?”

Every head turned.

Retta did not move. Her gloved fingers flexed once, just the hint of a curl, before relaxing.

Darian’s smile faltered.

Livia froze mid-step, sketchbook slipping from her arm. It hit the floor with a dull thud, pages fluttering. The crowd gasped. A server scrambled to retrieve it.

Retta stepped forward. The motion drew every eye—black silk against crimson wool, white lilies embroidered along her cuffs.

Livia bent to pick up the book, her fingers trembling. She didn’t meet Retta’s gaze.

Darian laughed—too sharp, too sudden. “Clumsy girl. These things happen.” He turned back to the dais, waving a hand. “Continue.”

But the moment had cracked.

Elders shifted in their seats. A woman in a sapphire brooch leaned toward her neighbor and whispered—too loudly—“She hasn’t spoken a word in six months. Not a single word.”

Retta heard. She felt the vibrations in the floorboards.

Livia straightened, sketchbook clutched to her chest. Her thumb brushed the pages. One corner of the leather cover had split, revealing a flash of silk thread inside—glinting silver against white.

Retta’s gaze locked on it.

Livia’s breath hitched. She tucked the book tighter, but not before one page fell open.

A half-finished embroidery stared up from the spread: tiny silver stitches curling like vines. A cipher. Retta’s cipher.

Not a random pattern. Not a swirl of flowers.

Her initials twisted through the threads.

Livia’s lips parted.

Retta took a step forward.

The room held its breath.

Then Livia’s fingers whitened around the sketchbook. A needle, forgotten in the embroidery, pricked her skin. A bead of blood welled at the tip of her thumb.

She didn’t wipe it away.

Darian finally noticed. He turned. “Livia, what—”

She snapped the book shut. Blood smeared the cover.

The needle clattered to the floor.

Silence.

Retta stopped three feet away. The scent of copper and silk hung between them.

Livia’s eyes—green, flecked with gold—locked onto Retta’s. Not with recognition. Not with betrayal.

With fear.

Her lips moved. Just once.

“Run,” she whispered.

Darian’s face darkened. “What did you say?”

Livia didn’t answer. She stepped back, heels catching on the hem of her gown. The sketchbook slipped from her grip and fell again, pages splaying.

Retta did not reach for it.

On the floor, the embroidery lay exposed. Silver thread glinted in the gaslight. The cipher was incomplete, but there was no mistaking the pattern. No mistaking the hand that had stitched it.

Darian’s jaw clenched.

Retta lifted her chin.

The dais gong sounded again—too loud, too late.

And for the first time in public, she spoke.

“You’re bleeding,” she said to Livia.

Then she turned and walked out of the Gilded Hive, her black skirts whispering over the marble like a promise.

chapters.title: **Chapter 8: The Bleed**
chapters.text:
**Chapter 8: The Bleed**

The dye master’s office smelled of sulfur and spoiled wine. His fingers trembled over a ledger stained with indigo, the numbers bleeding across the page like bruises.

Darian stood in the doorway, coat unbuttoned, cuffs rolled past the wrist. The gaslight flickered, casting shadows like jagged teeth across the walls.

“You were supposed to be burning that silver thread,” Darian said.

The dye master swallowed. “I was. But the order changed.”

“By whose authority?”

“Elio’s.”

Darian’s knuckles whitened around the ledger. The ink smelled wrong—too fresh, too deliberate. He flipped through the pages. Every entry had been altered. Smuggled silver. Missing invoices. Cross-outs inked in violet, a color only one person used.

His voice was quiet. “Where is he?”

The dye master didn’t answer. A knock came at the door.

Livia stood there, her skin too pale, her hands clutching a bloodstained handkerchief. “Darian, we have another problem.”

He didn’t look at her. “Not now.”

“It’s in the Frost Chapel.”

That cut through. He turned. “What?”

“Elder Corvino. He’s dead.”

---

The Frost Chapel sat at the heart of the Silver Towers, its marble floors veined with silver like a wound. The air was thick with incense and the scent of charred paper. Corvino lay sprawled across the dais, his burgundy sash askew, his ledger reduced to ash in a silver censer beside him.

Elio stood at the foot of the steps, face unreadable. His gloves were smudged with soot.

Darian stepped over the body. The censer was still warm. “How long?”

“Minutes,” Elio said.

Livia pushed past. She crouched by the censer, fingers hovering over the ashes. Her breath hitched. “No. That’s not possible.”

Darian grabbed her wrist. “What?”

She twisted free, but not before he saw the tremor in her hand. “The ledger’s cipher. It’s Retta’s.”

The words landed like a blade between ribs. Darian let go of her.

Elio exhaled slowly. “Burned to ash. Just like the others.”

Darian kicked the censer. The charcoal scattered, embers skittering across the marble. “Find her.”

---

The Marble Ledger House loomed ahead, its columns like skeletal fingers. Retta stood under the portico, her black veil pulled low. She had not worn it for mourning.

A servant approached, bowing. “Contessa. Marquise Elio requests your presence.”

She didn’t move. “Where?”

“His chambers.”

His chambers. Not the ledger room. Not the hall of elders. His private sanctuary.

Retta followed the servant up the curved staircase, her heels clicking against the stone. The air smelled of beeswax and old parchment. The walls were lined with portraits of long-dead Syndicate presidents, their eyes following her like ghosts.

Elio waited inside. The fire was low, casting long shadows. He stood by the window, hands clasped behind his back. A decanter of brandy sat untouched on the table.

“You asked for me,” she said.

He turned. His face was a mask of controlled calm. “Three crises at once. Darian’s dye master caught smuggling. Livia collapsed mid-ball. And now Corvino is dead—his ledger burned with your cipher in the ash.”

Retta didn’t react. She pulled off her gloves, folded them neatly.

Elio stepped closer. “This is no coincidence. You’ve been busy.”

The fire popped. A log shifted, sending up a shower of sparks.

“You don’t get to act surprised,” she said. “Not after what you did to my father. Not after you burned his ledgers and handed the ashes to Darian like a gift.”

His jaw tightened. “I protected you.”

“You buried me alive.”

Silence. The clock on the mantel ticked, slow and deliberate.

Elio poured brandy into two glasses. Handed her one. “Drink. Before you hear the rest.”

She set it down, untouched.

He drank his in one swallow. “Darian believes this chaos is bad luck. Livia thinks it’s proof of fraud. The elders whisper of treason. And Corvino’s death? That was meant to be me. I was supposed to review his ledger tonight.”

Retta’s pulse didn’t flicker. “Why tell me?”

“Because you’re the only one who can fix it.” He set the glass down with a sharp click. “Marry Darian. Tonight. Before the Syndicate convenes in the morning. Let them believe the ledgers were destroyed. Let them think the threat is gone.”

“And if I refuse?”

His voice dropped. “Then the ledger you smuggled out of the vault—the one with the insurance fraud—goes to the Syndicate’s ceremonial forge. Burned in front of every elder. Your name with it.”

The firelight caught the silver thread in the hem of her gown. It glinted, faint but unmistakable.

Elio saw it. His eyes darkened. “You’re not just stitching ledgers anymore, are you? You’re sewing a noose.”

Retta smiled. Just once. “I’m sewing justice.”

A knock at the door. A servant bowed in the hallway. “Marquise. The dye master’s been found. He’s confessed to smuggling silver thread across the northern route.”

Elio didn’t look away from Retta. “And?”

The servant hesitated. “He says it was under your orders.”

The decanter slipped from Elio’s hand. Brandy pooled on the rug, dark and spreading.

Retta stepped over it, heading for the door. “Tell Darian I’ll marry him. But not tonight.”

The servant’s eyes darted between them. “When, then?”

She paused at the threshold. “When I’m ready.”

The door closed behind her.

Elio stood in the spreading dark, listening to her footsteps fade down the hall. The brandy seeped into the rug like blood.

Then the servant whispered again, voice urgent.

“The vault’s panel’s been loosened,” the boy said. “Someone’s been there.”

chapters.title: **Chapter 9: The Offer**
chapters.text:
**Chapter 9: The Offer**

The Frost Chapel crypt was cold enough to preserve a corpse. Retta moved between the rows of silent stone, her skirts whispering against the marble like a mourner’s final prayer. The air smelled of damp and old roses—funeral wreaths left to rot. She paused before the crypt of her mother, one gloved hand resting on the stone. Not for sentiment. The ledger stitching in her sleeve was unfinished.

A door creaked behind her.

Elio stepped from the shadows, his coat dark as wet earth. He didn’t speak. Didn’t need to. The weight of his presence pressed against her spine.

She turned. “You asked for me.”

“I did.” His voice was low, measured. “And you came.”

“You underestimate me.”

He stepped closer, close enough that she could see the silver thread in the cuff of his coat—one she’d woven as a child. A gift. A secret. “Sit with me.”

A bench of black marble stood beneath a flickering sconce. They sat. The stone was cold through her skirts.

Elio poured brandy from a flask into two tarnished tin cups. Handed her one.

She didn’t drink.

He drank. Then wiped his mouth with the back of his hand. “You’ve done more than expose threads, Retta. You’ve unraveled the whole loom.”

“And you’ve been counting stitches.”

A flicker of irritation crossed his face. “Darian thinks you’re a ghost. Livia thinks you’re a forger. The elders think you’re a traitor.” He leaned forward. “But I know. You’re the true heir of Velsanti. Not just the name. The fortune. The proof.”

She didn’t react. Just waited.

He exhaled through his nose. “Help us fix this. Marry Darian. Play the grieving widow. Let the Syndicate believe the ledgers are ash. And in return… your name returns. Your seat on the council. Your honor.”

Retta turned the cup in her fingers. The brandy sloshed, dark and bitter. “And what about Elio D’Annunzio’s seat?”

His jaw tightened. “I’ll take the fall for the smuggling. For the fraud. For the fire.” A pause. “I’ll be disgraced. Ruined. But you’ll be free.”

“Free.” She tasted the word. It was sour. “And you? Will you retire to the countryside? Write your memoirs of a fallen marquise?”

He didn’t smile. “I’ll do what’s necessary.”

She set the cup down. It made no sound. “Why me?”

“Because you’re the only one who can stitch this back together.” He looked at her. Really looked. “You have the proof. The cipher. The ledgers. But you don’t have the heart to burn them.” A beat. “I do.”

Retta stood. The hem of her gown dragged across the stone. “And if I refuse?”

Elio stood too. His shadow swallowed hers. “Then the dye master’s confession becomes the Syndicate’s gospel. The elders will award the presidency to Darian by noon. The ceremonial forge will burn your proof in front of them all. And you? You’ll vanish. Not as Claretta Valente. But as a thief. A forger. A murderer’s daughter.”

She walked to the crypt’s iron gate. Ran her fingers along the cold bars. “You could have burned the ledgers yourself. Why involve me?”

“Because I needed you to *want* to be seen.”

Retta stilled. Then turned slowly. “What?”

Elio stepped into the dim light. “Everyone thinks you’re a ghost. A mourner. A body without a voice. But you’re stitching ledgers into your gown. Burning them in ballrooms. Smuggling evidence under their noses.” His voice dropped. “You want them to see you. To *know* you. Because fear is power. And you’ve hoarded it like a dragon on a hoard.”

She said nothing.

He reached into his coat. Not for a gun. For a folded sheet of paper. Retta’s cipher stamped in silver thread.

He held it out.

She didn’t take it.

Elio placed it on the bench between them. “Sign the betrothal papers. Tonight. Before the Syndicate gathers. Then your name returns. Your fortune is safe. And Elio D’Annunzio takes the blame.”

The paper lay between them like a contract with the devil.

Retta’s hand moved. Not to pick it up. To her sleeve. To the hidden seam where the final stitch waited.

She didn’t look at him as she slid the silver thread from her cuff. A cuff link he’d given her when she was twelve. Engraved with the Velsanti crest.

She dropped it into his palm.

Elio stared at it. The silver gleamed dully in his hand.

Then Retta walked away.

She didn’t look back.

Elio stood in the crypt’s cold, watching the door swing shut behind her. The cuff link burned in his fist like a brand.

Then the door creaked again.

Not from her return.

From behind.

A figure stood at the far end of the crypt. Black silk. A veil that didn’t hide the eyes beneath.

Livia Sable.

Her hands were empty. But her gaze locked onto Elio’s. Unblinking. Unforgiving.

And the cuff link in his palm felt suddenly like a noose.

Chapter 10

The Obsidian Vault was not a room. It was a mouth.

Retta stepped inside and the door groaned shut behind her, sealing out the gaslit hum of the Marble Ledger House. The air smelled of aged paper and clove oil—Elio’s scent, though he had not come. The darkness wrapped around her like a second gown, cool and suffocating. She lit a single taper from a hidden sconce.

The walls breathed.

Rows of ledger panels stretched from floor to ceiling, each framed in black iron. Not wood. Not cloth. Iron. Because paper could burn. Iron could not. The pages were not bound in leather—they were stitched. Silk thread, silver filament, copper wire. Names embroidered in gold, in red, in the deep violet of crushed amethyst. Some pages still bled ink from fresh stitches. Others were yellowed, their threads brittle as old bone.

She ran her fingers along the spines. Each one hummed with a voice she knew.

“You see names,” she murmured, “but these are ledgers of silence.”

She pressed her palm to the nearest panel. The threads prickled against her skin. Not from heat. From memory.

A ledger never lies.

But it can forget.

Retta moved deeper into the vault, her skirts whispering over the iron grates. The floor was metal too—perforated, so that cold air rose from the city’s forgotten underbelly. She had built this room in sections, over years, smuggling panels in through the frost tunnels beneath the Frost Chapel. Elio had called it her “grieving project.” He had no idea it was her arsenal.

She stopped before the central altar—a marble plinth carved with the Velsanti crest. On it lay the unfinished ledger: black silk stretched over oak, needles poised like daggers. The final page was a question mark in thread.

She picked up a needle. Pierced the silk.

Stitched.

The needle moved on its own.

Not from skill. From memory. The page filled with names: Orsini. Corvino. D’Annunzio. Sable. Each name a knot. Each knot a truth. Or a lie. She didn’t care yet.

She set the needle down.

Then she saw it.

A gap. A missing panel. Not torn out. Removed.

Her breath hitched.

The stitching was fresh. Too fresh. Gold thread. Her cipher. But not hers. Someone else’s hand.

She knelt. Ran her fingers over the scar in the wall. A ledger had been extracted. Not by force—by needle. Someone had unstitched it. Carefully. Like removing a tooth.

Her cipher glinted in the lamplight.

Livia Sable.

Retta stood.

She walked to the opposite wall. Another gap. Another fresh seam. Another ledger stolen.

Her pulse throbbed in her throat.

She moved faster now, scanning the names. The Syndicate’s secrets were bleeding out. Not burned. Not destroyed. Unstitched.

Retta’s fingers curled into fists.

A sound.

Footsteps.

Soft. Deliberate. Not Elio. Too light. Too quick.

She extinguished the taper.

Darkness swallowed her.

The footsteps paused.

Then—

A knife of light cut through the gloom. A lantern bobbed into view.

Retta pressed herself into the iron paneling, her black silk dissolving into the void.

A figure stepped forward.

Livia Sable.

She wore a high-collared blouse of dove gray, sleeves rolled to the elbow. Her hands were bare—no gloves. No rings. No distractions. In one hand, she carried a lantern. In the other, a pair of silver scissors.

Her eyes flicked across the walls.

She saw the gaps.

Saw the scars.

She exhaled sharply through her nose.

Then she giggled.

A sound so unexpected, so unguarded, that Retta flinched.

Livia pressed a hand to her mouth, stifling it. But the laugh escaped anyway—a girl’s laugh, bright and reckless.

She walked to the nearest gap. Touched the raw edge of the silk. The scissors gleamed.

“You’re stitching in reverse,” Livia said. “That’s not how you win.”

Retta said nothing.

Livia turned. The lantern light painted her face in gold. She didn’t scream. Didn’t gasp. Just looked at Retta, as if she’d known she was there all along.

“You’re the ghost in the gown,” Livia said. “The Black Silk Widow. The silent heiress. The forger in mourning.”

Retta did not deny it.

Livia stepped closer. “I found your cipher on a page in the Gilded Hive. Stitched into a seam allowance. Almost invisible.” She tilted her head. “Almost.”

Silence stretched.

“You’re stealing my ledgers,” Retta said.

Livia smiled. “No. I’m finishing yours.” She held up the scissors. “These are the only true needles now. The only threads that cut.”

Retta’s gaze flicked to the scissors. To Livia’s hands.

“You want to burn it all,” Retta said. “Burn the ledgers. Burn the Syndicate. Burn Darian.”

Livia’s smile faded. “He burned our father’s ledgers. Let the fire claim everything. I refuse to let him do it again.” She stepped closer, lantern light trembling. “You think you’re the only one with a cipher? I have one too. Mine says *fire*. And I’ve stitched it into everything he owns.”

Retta felt the words like a blade between her ribs.

Livia’s gaze dropped to Retta’s cuff. To the single silver thread woven into the black silk.

Not a seam.

A ledger.

A name.

Livia’s hand shot out. She seized Retta’s wrist.

“You,” she whispered. “You were in the vault that night. When the fire happened. You stitched the insurance fraud into the marquise’s coat. You *finished* the ledger he tried to destroy.”

Retta did not pull away.

Livia’s fingers tightened. “Then you know what’s at stake.”

Retta met her eyes.

The lantern flickered.

In the sudden dim, a page at Retta’s feet crumbled.

Not from age. From air. From heat. From something inside it.

Livia gasped.

A single page had fallen from one of the stolen ledgers. It lay on the iron grate, curling at the edges. The ink bled. The thread smoked.

Then—

It ignited.

A blue flame, no higher than a candle, danced across the page.

Livia dropped her lantern. It clattered, glass shattering. The flame did not die. It spread.

Not upward. Sideways. Along the iron grate.

Toward the gaps in the wall.

Toward the unfinished ledger.

Retta lunged.

She seized the burning page and crushed it in her fist.

Ash rained from her fingers.

But the flame did not stop.

It had already reached the next gap.

And the next.

The silk panels began to smoke.

Livia stumbled back, eyes wide. “What have you done?”

Retta looked at the spreading fire.

Then at Livia.

And said, quietly:

“You finished my ledgers in the wrong order.”

The fire licked the hem of Livia’s skirt.

Chapter 11

The Marble Ledger House smelled of regret and wet wool.

Elio pushed through the brass doors, his coat damp from the winter air. The Syndicate’s inner chamber echoed with raised voices, the kind that carried weight in this city of silver tongues. He didn’t look back. He didn’t need to.

The vote had gone against Darian.

The loss echoed in the hollows of his chest.

He took the servants’ stairs, the ones meant for clerks and messengers, not marquises. His boots clicked on the marble like a metronome counting down his ruin. The Syndicate presidency wasn’t just a title—it was a noose, and Darian had just tightened it around his neck.

Elio reached the ground floor. The grand lobby yawned before him, all polished stone and gaslight. A clerk bustled past, arms laden with ledgers. He didn’t stop. Didn’t ask. Didn’t care.

The doors to Darian’s office stood at the end of the hall, mahogany and imposing. Elio didn’t knock. He pushed inside.

Darian sat behind his desk, fingers steepled, face shadowed by the desk lamp. The marriage contract lay before him, half-burned. Flame had licked the edges, turning contracts to ash. The room smelled of scorched parchment and something darker.

“You lost,” Elio said.

Darian didn’t look up. “You knew it was coming.”

“I knew *you* were coming.” Elio tossed his coat onto the visitor’s chair. “You let the vote turn. You let them see your weakness.”

“Or perhaps,” Darian said, finally lifting his gaze, “I let them see yours.” He tapped the burned contract. “You thought you were playing both sides, Elio. But you’re not a player. You’re a pawn. And I’ve moved you off the board.”

Elio’s jaw tightened. “The ledgers—”

“Are gone.” Darian leaned back. “Or so you believe.”

A lie. A deflection. A game.

Elio wanted to smash the lamp. Wanted to see the glass shatter. Wanted to feel the cut of it in his skin. But he only said, “You don’t burn what you need.”

Darian smiled. “Then you haven’t been listening.”

The door behind Elio creaked open.

Livia stood there, her face pale under the gaslight. Her sleeves were rolled to the elbows, her fingers stained with ink and something darker—burns. She clutched a shawl close to her chest, the fringe trembling.

Darian’s smile vanished. “Get out.”

Livia didn’t move. “I found it.”

A pause. Heavy. Suffocating.

“Found what?” Darian asked, voice low.

“The cipher.” Livia stepped forward, the shawl fluttering like a surrender flag. “Retta’s cipher. Not on a page. Not in a ledger. On *this*.”

She unfurled the shawl.

Gold thread glinted in the lamplight. A pattern woven into the silk. The same cipher Retta had stitched into her mourning gowns. The same cipher that marked ledgers of fire and fraud.

Darian’s fingers twitched.

Elio watched, still. Calculating.

“Where did you get this?” Darian asked.

“From her room.” Livia’s voice was steady, but her hands weren’t. “She left it on the rack. Embroidered in the hem. *Your* wedding gift, brother. She wanted you to see.”

Darian stood. The chair screeched. “You took it from her?”

“I took it for *you*.” Livia stepped closer. “She’s stitching your ruin into every seam. The shawl. The veil. The contract. She’s not mourning. She’s *arming*.”

Darian’s breath was a hiss. “You should have burned it.”

“With what?” Livia’s voice cracked. “With *you*?”

A silence so thick it could have suspended time.

Then—

Darian moved.

He seized the shawl from Livia’s hands. His grip was brutal. The gold thread bit into the silk. He held it up, examining the cipher. His face darkened.

“You let her sew this?” he asked Livia.

“No.” Livia’s voice was barely a whisper. “She didn’t. *I* did.”

Darian froze.

Elio’s chest tightened.

Livia’s eyes burned. “I stitched it myself. Every thread. Every knot. I wanted you to see. I wanted you to *know*.”

Darian’s fingers loosened their grip. The shawl fell from his hands, pooling on the desk like a discarded promise.

“You?” he said.

Livia didn’t answer. She didn’t need to.

The truth hung between them, heavy as the lead in a vault.

Darian’s face twisted. Not anger. Not shock. Something worse.

Betrayal.

He turned to Elio. “You knew.”

Elio didn’t flinch. “I knew she was stitching something. But I didn’t know it was for *you*.”

Darian’s hands clenched into fists. “You let this happen.”

“No,” Elio said, quiet and final. “You did.”

The room spun.

Darian grabbed the burned marriage contract. He crumpled it in his fist. “The auction is in three weeks. The Syndicate will not forget this betrayal.”

He looked at Livia. “Especially not *you*.”

Livia didn’t look away.

Darian strode past Elio, his shoulder brushing against him like a blade. The door slammed behind him, the sound echoing through the hall.

Silence.

Livia’s breath shuddered. She didn’t meet Elio’s eyes.

“What have you done?” he asked.

She picked up the shawl. Her fingers traced the gold thread. “What I had to.”

Elio’s gaze flickered to the shawl. To the cipher. To the ledger of retribution woven into every stitch.

“Darian won’t forgive this,” he said.

Livia’s voice was steel and silk. “He doesn’t have to forgive. He has to *fear*.”

Elio exhaled through his nose. “You’ve lit the fuse, Livia. But you don’t know what’s at the other end.”

She met his gaze. “I do. It’s *her*.”

A current of something electric passed between them. Not warmth. Not comfort. Something raw. Something dangerous.

Elio wanted to reach out. Wanted to pull her back from the edge she’d leapt from.

But he didn’t.

He only said, “She’ll see you.”

Livia’s fingers tightened on the shawl. “Let her.”

Elio turned toward the door. “You should go. Before Darian returns.”

Livia didn’t move. “What about you?”

“I’m already ruined,” he said. “But you? You’re standing in the flames.”

He left her there.

The Marble Ledger House hummed with the silence of a cathedral after the sermon. The Syndicate’s secrets were bleeding into the air. The shawl lay on the desk, the gold thread glinting like a warning.

Elio didn’t look back.

He had his own ledger to burn.

Chapter 12

The Marble Ledger House was quiet when Retta arrived. The Syndicate had adjourned for the day, the ledger rooms locked, the clerks gone. Only gas lamps and shadows remained, flickering like dying embers in the grand hall.

She moved through the lobby with the ease of a woman who had walked these halls a hundred times. But tonight, her boots didn’t whisper against the marble. They struck, sharp and deliberate. Each step a sentence she didn’t need to say aloud.

The door to Darian’s office was ajar. Not enough to announce herself. Just enough to let the light spill into the hall.

She pushed it open with her gloved palm.

Darian sat behind his desk, the burned marriage contract spread before him like a funeral shroud. His fingers traced the scorched edges, as if reading the ash. The lamplight carved deep shadows into the hollows of his face.

He didn’t look up.

“You always did prefer ruin to repair,” Retta said.

Darian’s hand stilled. His dark eyes lifted. They met hers. Not with recognition. With calculation. “You’re not welcome here.”

“Funny. I was just thinking the same of you.”

He exhaled through his nose. A sound like controlled fury. “The Syndicate meeting is over.”

“For you, perhaps. For me, it’s just beginning.”

She closed the door behind her. The latch clicked. A final punctuation.

Darian’s gaze flicked to the veil draped over her shoulders. Black silk, weighted with jet beads, the hem frayed where she had torn it from the gown. A gesture. A statement.

“Take it off,” he said.

“Why? So you can pretend you don’t already know who I am?”

His jaw tightened. “You signed it in blood. I read the contract.”

“You read a forgery.” Retta stepped forward. Her skirts whispered against the marble. “Every line stitched with my cipher. Every ledger forged in my handwriting. Every signature burned into a ghost.”

Darian’s fingers curled into fists. “You think your little games mean something here?”

“They mean everything.” She stopped at the edge of his desk. Just out of reach. Close enough to see the pulse jump in his throat. “You kept Elio’s forged insurance payout. The one that paid your father’s fire claim in full. The one that proved the blaze was arson dressed as accident. You buried it in the Syndicate archives. But you forgot one thing.”

She reached into the fold of her veil. Not for a weapon. For paper.

She laid the burned contract on his desk.

Darian’s breath caught.

It wasn’t the ash that made him falter. It was the stitches. The gold thread glinting along the edges like veins of ore. The same patterns Retta had woven into her mourning gowns. The same cipher that marked her ledgers of fire and fraud.

Darian’s fingers trembled as he touched the page.

“This wasn’t burned,” he said, voice rough. “This was *stitched*.”

“Every seam. Every knot. Every ledger entry.” Retta leaned in. The scent of lavender and lamp oil clung to her. “You thought Elio forged these records. But he only moved them. I made them.”

“You?” The word was a blade dragged across stone.

“Your fire wasn’t an accident. It was a fraud. Your father didn’t lose everything. He *gained*. He took a fortune in insurance payouts, laundered it through offshore trusts, and left the Syndicate to rot. And Elio? He helped. For thirty pieces of silver.”

Darian’s hand closed around the contract. Crumpled it. Burned his palm. He didn’t flinch.

“And you let me believe—”

“That your empire was built on ashes?” Retta smiled. Not warm. Not cruel. Just honest. “I let you believe what was convenient.”

A silence fell. Heavy. Not with guilt. With the weight of a ledger finally balanced.

Darian’s voice dropped. “What do you want?”

“Justice.” Retta reached up. Her fingers found the edge of her veil. One tug. One motion.

The black silk parted.

Her face emerged. Pale. Sharp. Not hidden in mourning. Not hidden in shadow. Just herself.

Darian’s breath stopped.

His gaze locked onto her eyes. Not with recognition. With shock. With something darker.

“Claretta?”

“Not for long.” Her voice was quiet. Final. “I’m reclaiming what’s mine. The Velsanti fortune. The Syndicate presidency. The truth.”

Darian’s hand fell from the contract. It fluttered to his desk like a dying bird.

“You’re not a widow,” he said. Not a question. A realization.

“No.” Retta let the veil fall back into place. “I’m the heiress you tried to erase.”

The door behind her creaked open.

Darian’s eyes flickered past her shoulder.

Livia stood there. Her coat damp from the winter air. Her gloves still on. Her face pale under the gaslight.

Her gaze met Retta’s.

And in that moment, she saw it. Not just the unveiled face. Not just the woman who had haunted her stitches for months.

She saw the cipher trembling in Retta’s grip.

The same gold thread. The same knots. The same ledger hidden in plain sight.

Livia’s breath hitched.

Her fingers twitched at her sides.

And Retta knew.

Knew without words.

Knew without doubt.

Livia had stitched this path. Every thread. Every knot.

And now, she saw the face behind the hand that wove it.

Chapter 13

The Marble Ledger House smelled of polished stone and old secrets. Retta ascended the grand staircase without glancing at the gilded frieze of weavers and merchants that lined the walls. Her gloves were black silk, the fingers too long—hiding what she carried.

At the top, double doors led to the Syndicate’s inner sanctum. Voices bled through the oak, tense and familiar.

She pushed inside.

Darian stood by the hearth, a glass of brandy forgotten in his hand. Flames licked at his cuffs but he didn’t flinch. Livia sat in an armchair, a ledger open on her lap. Her silver thimble glinted as she turned a page.

Both looked up.

Darian’s face darkened. “This is a private meeting.”

Livia closed the ledger. “It was.”

Retta didn’t stop. She walked to the desk, set down the envelope she carried. The flap wasn’t sealed.

Darian’s eyes flicked to it. “You’re late.”

“You still set the time.” She folded her hands. “I wanted to be sure.”

Livia’s fingers tightened around the ledger. “Sure of what?”

“Of who’s lying.” Retta tapped the envelope. “Elio’s forged insurance payout. The one that paid your father’s fire claim. The one that proves the blaze wasn’t an accident but a fraud.”

Silence.

Darian’s glass hit the desk. Brandy sloshed over the rim. He didn’t wipe it. “You don’t have that.”

“I do.” Retta slid the envelope across the mahogany. “Every forged signature. Every altered line. Every entry stitched with Elio’s cipher. But he made one mistake.” She leaned forward. “He used your cousin’s name to launder the money. A cousin no one’s ever met. A cousin who doesn’t exist.”

Livia’s breath caught.

Darian reached for the envelope. Retta let him take it. He pulled out the page. His eyes scanned it. His jaw locked.

Livia stood. The ledger slipped from her lap, thudded to the floor. She didn’t pick it up.

“Luciano Sable,” she said softly. “My cousin.”

Retta didn’t answer. She watched Livia’s face.

Darian’s voice was raw. “This isn’t possible.”

“It is.” Retta kept her voice even. “Your father’s fire insurance paid double the loss. The extra went to an offshore trust. Elio controls it now. But the beneficiary was listed as Luciano Sable—a man who died in Naples when he was five.”

Livia’s hands clenched. “You’re wrong.”

“Am I?” Retta took a step closer. “You recognized my cipher on the ledgers. You thought I was copying you. But I wasn’t. I was erasing Elio. And you helped.”

Darian’s fingers crushed the page. “Livia, what is she talking about?”

Livia didn’t look at him. Her gaze stayed on Retta. “I thought you were the fraud. The one trying to ruin us. I stitched my cipher into that shawl to trace you. To prove it was you.”

“And I let you.” Retta’s voice softened, just enough. “Because I needed you to see. To know the truth doesn’t hide in shadows.”

Livia’s lips parted. Closed. Opened again. “I didn’t know it was *you*.”

Retta held her gaze. “You didn’t want to.”

The words hung. Not an accusation. Not an insult. A fact.

Darian dropped the page. It fluttered to the floor like a dead leaf. “This changes nothing.”

Livia finally looked at him. Her eyes were bright. “It changes everything.”

Retta watched them. The brother who had built an empire on ash. The sister who had stitched her own guilt into lace.

Darian’s voice was low. Controlled. Deadly. “You expect me to believe this is justice?”

Retta smiled. Not triumphant. Just certain. “I expect you to believe in arithmetic.”

Livia’s fingers brushed the ledger on the floor. Her thimble caught the lamplight.

Darian exhaled through his teeth. “You’re no better than us.”

Retta didn’t answer.

She smiled.

And waited.

Chapter 14

The Frost Chapel smelled of candle wax and frozen stone. Retta stood at the altar, her black silk gown pooling like spilled ink across the marble steps. Behind her, the Syndicate elders arranged themselves in crescent rows, their fur-lined coats whispering against the pews. Darian waited at the foot of the steps, his gloves clutched too tight.

No one spoke.

Retta lifted the envelope from her sleeve. The ledgers inside were dry, but the air felt damp. She held them out, not to Darian, but to the air between them.

“These are the terms,” she said.

Darian’s jaw worked. “Terms?”

“For your surrender.”

A ripple passed through the elders. One coughed. Another adjusted his spectacles.

Darian’s voice was a blade unsheathed. “You have no power here.”

Retta didn’t raise her voice. “I have every ledger. Every forged signature. Every transaction Elio funneled through your cousin’s ghost. You want to talk about power? I own the proof of your fire. The proof of your fraud. The proof that you and your sister have been bleeding the Syndicate dry for years.”

Livia stood at the back of the chapel, her violet shawl draped over her shoulders like a second skin. The silver thread in the hem caught the lamplight, shifting as she moved. She didn’t come forward.

Darian’s eyes flickered toward her. “Livia—”

“Don’t.” Her voice was quiet. Firm. “Not here.”

The elders shifted in their seats. The sound was like dry leaves skittering.

Retta stepped closer. The hem of her gown brushed the altar cloth. “You have two choices. One: exile. A pension. A quiet life somewhere south, where the silk merchants won’t remember your name. Or two: public trial. Your fire insurance fraud exposed. Your father’s ghost dragged through the courts. Your sister’s name dragged through them too, because she stitched her guilt into every edge of this empire.”

Darian’s hands curled into fists. “You’re bluffing.”

Retta opened the envelope. She didn’t pull out the ledgers. She pulled out a single sheet, folded small. She held it up. The paper trembled, just enough.

“This is the inventory list for the auction house,” she said. “Twenty-one days from now. The ledgers I burned tonight? They were just a fraction. I left this behind. And I left it with the one person in this room who still has a conscience.”

A long silence.

Livia’s fingers twitched at her sides. Not toward her brother. Not toward Retta. Like she was measuring the distance.

Darian’s breath came fast. “You think this makes you queen?”

Retta folded the paper back into the envelope. She slid it across the altar step. It stopped an inch from his polished boot.

“No,” she said. “It makes me president.”

The elders inhaled. A collective, shallow gasp.

Darian’s face darkened. “You can’t—”

“I can.” Retta met his stare. “I’m Claretta Velsanti. Sole heir to the northern silver mines, the Valenta trade route, and whatever fortune your father stole to build his empire. The Syndicate charter names me as the rightful president. The elders know it. They’ve known it for weeks, since I stitched my claim into the auction ledger.”

One elder, a gaunt man with a silver-tipped cane, nodded once. A single, slow motion.

Darian’s voice was low. Deadly. “You’re giving me exile.”

Retta smiled. Not the smile of a bride. The smile of a woman who had already won.

“No,” she said. “I’m giving you a choice. Take the pension. Leave the city. Or stand here and watch your name dragged through the mud while your sister testifies against you.”

Livia’s shawl shifted again. The silver thread glinted.

Darian’s mouth twisted. “And if I refuse?”

Retta looked past him, at the frozen saints carved into the chapel walls.

“Then I start with the auction house,” she said. “Then the fire. Then every ledger your family ever touched. By dawn, the entire Syndicate will know you’re a fraud. By noon, your factories will be picketed. By nightfall, your name will be a joke in every tavern from here to the border.”

Darian’s glove struck the envelope. It skidded across the marble, thudded against an elder’s boot.

The elder didn’t pick it up.

Livia stepped forward. One pace. Two.

The chapel was silent.

She didn’t look at Retta. She looked at her brother.

“Darian,” she said. “It’s over.”

His face twisted. “You’d let her do this?”

Livia’s hands stayed at her sides. “I’d let her save us.”

Darian exhaled through his teeth. The sound was raw. Animal. He spat on the marble floor between them.

“You’re no better than us,” he said.

Retta watched the saliva darken the stone.

Then she smiled.

And waited.

Chapter 15

The Gilded Hive’s war room smelled of polished brass and damp wool. Retta stood at the head of the mahogany table, her violet silk dress pooling around the chair like spilled wine. The Syndicate elders filled the room, their coats still dusted with frost from the chapel. One coughed into a handkerchief. Another tapped a silver-tipped cane against the floor.

Livia worked at the far end of the table, stitching silver thread into a length of cream silk. The needle moved with deliberate precision, catching the light like a flicker of ice.

Retta didn’t look at her.

“You understand the risks,” she said to the elders.

The gaunt man with the cane adjusted his spectacles. “Publicly, yes. But internally?”

“Internally,” Retta said, “I control the ledgers. The auctions. The trade routes. Darian’s exile is signed, sealed, and witnessed. The moment he steps off the steamship tomorrow, his name is scrubbed from every Syndicate record.”

A murmur rippled through the room. One elder cleared his throat. “And the cipher? The one in your gown?”

Retta’s fingers brushed the hem of her skirt. The silver thread there was delicate, nearly invisible. “It’s not a cipher anymore. It’s a signature. Mine.”

Livia’s needle stilled. A single drop of blood welled at her fingertip, dark against the cream silk. She didn’t flinch. Didn’t wipe it away.

One of the elders leaned forward. “Madam President, the ledgers you’ve recovered—Elio’s offshore trusts, the fire insurance fraud—are they secure?”

“Secure enough.” Retta kept her voice even. “They’re locked in the Obsidian Vault. The key is in three separate pockets. Two men and a widow hold the shares.”

Livia’s lips pressed together. A flicker of something—amusement? Respect?—crossed her face. Then the needle found purchase again, stitching deeper into the silk.

Retta watched the blood bloom.

“You’re bleeding,” she said.

Livia didn’t look up. “So I am.”

A silence settled. Not uncomfortable. Measured.

Retta stepped closer. The elders shifted, making room.

“You recognize the stitch,” Retta said.

Livia’s fingers didn’t pause. “I do.”

“Then you know what it means.”

Livia finally looked up. Her eyes were sharp. Calculating. “I know what it means for you.”

Retta leaned against the table. The mahogany was cold beneath her palms. “And for you?”

Livia’s needle pricked the silk again. A clean, deliberate motion. The blood welled, bright and quick.

She smiled.

“For me,” Livia said, “it means you’re exactly what you claimed to be.”

The elders shifted in their seats. One cleared his throat. Another adjusted his gloves.

Retta didn’t blink. “And?”

Livia set the needle down. The silk lay flat, the silver thread catching the lamplight like a web of ice.

“And,” she said, “it means I should have burned your ledgers when I had the chance.”

Retta’s fingers twitched at her sides. Not toward her sleeve. Not toward the ledgers in her coat pocket. Toward the chair.

“You had the chance,” Retta said. “You didn’t.”

Livia picked up the silk. Rolled it carefully, the silver thread glinting in the lamplight. “No,” she said. “I didn’t.”

The elders were watching now. All of them. Waiting.

Retta’s voice was low. “Why?”

Livia folded the silk into a neat square. Tucked it beneath her arm. “Because I wanted to see you win.”

A beat of silence.

Retta exhaled. “You could have told him. Years ago.”

Livia’s smile didn’t waver. “And let him ruin us both?”

Retta almost laughed. Almost.

“Us?” she said.

Livia’s gaze flickered toward the elders. Then back to Retta.

“You and me,” she said. “The ones who stitch the truth into the edges.”

Retta’s hand settled on the back of the chair. The wood was smooth beneath her palm.

“What do you want, Livia?”

Livia tucked a loose strand of hair behind her ear. The movement was precise. Deliberate.

“A seat at the table,” she said.

Retta didn’t answer right away. The elders rustled. One shuffled his papers.

“You’re Darian’s sister,” Retta said.

Livia’s fingers tightened on the silk. “I’m the lead designer of Rook & Sable Textiles. I’m the one who recognized your cipher before you ever stitched it. I’m the one who could have burned your ledgers in the hearth of the Gilded Hive.”

Retta’s pulse thrummed in her throat. “And now?”

Livia stepped closer. The space between them was narrow. Filled with the scent of silk and blood.

“Now,” Livia said, “I want to be your partner.”

Retta’s fingers curled into the chair. The wood creaked.

“Why?”

Livia’s gaze dropped to Retta’s mouth. A beat. Then her eyes lifted.

“Because,” she said, “I want to see what you do next.”

The elders were holding their breaths. The air in the room was thick, heavy with the scent of wool and polish.

Retta’s voice was barely above a whisper.

“And if I say no?”

Livia’s needle lay on the table between them. Silver. Delicate. Deadly.

She smiled again.

“Then,” she said, “I’ll stitch your ruin into every scarf I design.”

The chair beneath Retta’s hand was cold.

And for the first time since the Frost Chapel, she shivered.
